title: Why Is My Cat Scooting After Urinating? | Veterinary Advice
meta_desc: Learn why your cat is scooting after using the litter box, common causes like anal gland issues, and when to contact your veterinarian for help.
content_html:
What is Cat Scooting?

Scooting is a behavior where a cat drags their hindquarters across the floor, often on carpeted surfaces. While it can look unusual, it is a cat's way of trying to relieve localized itching, irritation, or pressure in the anal or urinary area. When it happens specifically after urination, it suggests the cat is feeling a lingering sensation or discomfort in their lower urinary tract or surrounding tissues.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for scooting is generally Low. In most cases, it indicates a chronic or mild irritation rather than an immediate life-threatening crisis. However, you should monitor your cat for other symptoms. If your cat is unable to pass any urine, is straining repeatedly in the litter box, or is vocalizing in pain, these are signs of a medical emergency and you should contact an emergency vet immediately.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a clear, well-lit photo of your cat’s perianal area (the area under the tail) can be very helpful for a veterinary triage professional. A photo allows them to see if there is visible inflammation, swelling of the anal glands, discharge, or signs of parasites like tapeworms. This visual evidence helps the vet determine whether your cat needs an immediate exam or if the issue can be managed with a scheduled routine appointment.

faqs.question: Can intestinal parasites cause scooting?
faqs.answer: Yes, certain parasites like tapeworms can cause significant itching around the anus as segments are passed, leading to scooting behavior.

faqs.question: What are anal glands and why do they cause scooting?
faqs.answer: Anal glands are small sacs on either side of the anus that produce a scent. If these sacs become full, impacted, or infected, the cat may scoot to try and express them.

faqs.question: Does scooting mean my cat has a UTI?
faqs.answer: It can be a secondary sign. Irritation from a urinary tract infection or feline lower urinary tract disease (FLUTD) can cause a cat to feel discomfort in that region after they finish urinating.
